jsonContent.title: SkinApp Training Final Assessment
jsonContent.text:
Final Assessment

You will now complete a comprehensive assessment covering all modules:

  • Module 1: Project Overview
  • Module 2: Clinical Photography
  • Module 3: Informed Consent
  • Module 4: Privacy & Anonymization
  • Module 5: Safeguarding

You need to achieve 80% to pass. You can retake this assessment if needed.

params.question: What is the PRIMARY goal of the WHO SkinNTD AI Image Collection Initiative?

answers.text: To train AI systems for improved leprosy and skin NTD diagnosis
answers.tip: Correct! The initiative aims to build AI diagnostic tools to help healthcare workers in resource-limited settings.

answers.text: To replace dermatologists with AI technology
answers.tip: Incorrect. The AI is designed to support, not replace, healthcare professionals.

answers.text: To create a commercial database of skin images
answers.tip: Incorrect. This is a non-commercial public health initiative.

answers.text: To develop new medications for leprosy
answers.tip: Incorrect. The focus is on diagnostic support, not drug development.

params.question: Which metadata field is used to document skin tone for AI diversity purposes?

answers.text: Clinical Form (Ridley-Jopling Classification)
answers.tip: Incorrect. This classifies the type and severity of leprosy.

answers.text: Phototype (Fitzpatrick Scale)
answers.tip: Correct! The Fitzpatrick Scale (I-VI) documents skin tone to ensure AI works across diverse populations.

answers.text: Operational Classification
answers.tip: Incorrect. This categorizes paucibacillary vs multibacillary for treatment.

answers.text: Reaction Status
answers.tip: Incorrect. This documents whether the patient has leprosy reactions.

params.question: A patient can withdraw consent at any time, even during photography.
params.tip: Correct! Consent is an ongoing process, not a one-time event. Patients can withdraw at any moment.

params.question: Which of the following should be removed from photos before submission?

answers.text: The clinical lesion being documented
answers.tip: Incorrect. The lesion itself should be clearly visible as it's the subject of documentation.

answers.text: Patient's hospital wristband
answers.tip: Correct! Wristbands contain identifying information and must be removed or covered.

answers.text: Clinical context surrounding the lesion
answers.tip: Incorrect. Surrounding skin provides important clinical context and should remain visible.

answers.text: A ruler placed for scale
answers.tip: Incorrect. Scale indicators like rulers or coins are helpful for size reference.

params.question: When photographing lesions on private body areas, which is NOT required?

answers.text: Specific consent for that body region
answers.tip: Incorrect. Explicit consent for the specific body area IS required.

answers.text: Presence of a chaperone
answers.tip: Incorrect. Offering a chaperone is recommended practice.

answers.text: Photographing the entire body for context
answers.tip: Correct! You should photograph only the necessary area. Full body photos are NOT required and should be avoided.

answers.text: Professional draping
answers.tip: Incorrect. Proper draping IS required to maintain dignity.

params.question: If a patient seems confused about what they're consenting to, you should proceed quickly before they change their mind.
params.tip: Incorrect! Confusion is a red flag. You must ensure understanding before proceeding. When in doubt, take more time to explain.

params.question: A patient speaks a different language and no interpreter is available. A family member offers to translate. What should you do?

answers.text: Accept the family member's translation
answers.tip: Incorrect. Family translation is NOT sufficient for informed consent.

answers.text: Use simple gestures and assume consent
answers.tip: Incorrect. This is inadequate for complex medical consent.

answers.text: Wait for a professional interpreter or phone translation service
answers.tip: Correct! Professional interpretation ensures accuracy and patient autonomy.

answers.text: Proceed without consent since it's for a good cause
answers.tip: Incorrect. Consent is mandatory regardless of the project's importance.

params.question: You notice signs of possible child abuse while reviewing photos. What should you do?

answers.text: Submit the photo as evidence
answers.tip: Incorrect. Do NOT submit images showing signs of abuse.

answers.text: Ignore it and focus on the skin condition
answers.tip: Incorrect. You have a duty to report suspected abuse.

answers.text: Report to your institution's child protection protocol immediately
answers.tip: Correct! Follow your institution's safeguarding protocols immediately.

answers.text: Ask the parent about the bruises first
answers.tip: Incorrect. Follow institutional protocols. Don't investigate on your own.

params.question: The governance structure includes WHO, TLM International, Partner Organizations, and Clinicians, with clinicians having direct responsibility to patients.
params.tip: Correct! You are part of Level 3-4 and have direct responsibility to ensure patient welfare.

params.question: Which of these is a 'red flag' indicating consent may not be voluntary?

answers.text: Patient asks detailed questions about the project
answers.tip: Incorrect. Asking questions indicates engagement and understanding.

answers.text: Patient looks to family member before answering
answers.tip: Correct! Looking to others for approval may indicate pressure or lack of autonomy.

answers.text: Patient takes time to read the consent form
answers.tip: Incorrect. Reading carefully shows responsible decision-making.

answers.text: Patient requests clarification on data usage
answers.tip: Incorrect. Questions about data show appropriate concern.

overallFeedback.feedback: You did not pass. You need 80% to complete this training. Please review the modules and try again.

Congratulations! You have successfully completed the SkinApp Training Course. You are now prepared to:

  • Collect high-quality clinical images
  • Obtain proper informed consent
  • Maintain patient dignity and privacy
  • Identify and respond to safeguarding concerns

Remember: Patient welfare always comes first. When in doubt, stop and consult.
